sourceMovie.title: The Wolf of Wall Street
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit The Wolf of Wall Street for the same reason it broke through in 2013: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a drama rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Martin Scorsese keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: War Dogs
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Wolf of Wall Street clicked for you, War Dogs (2016) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: American Psycho
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Wolf of Wall Street clicked for you, American Psycho (2000) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Wolf of Wall Street?
faq.answer: Try War Dogs, American Psycho, The Big Short—each shares DNA with The Wolf of Wall Street while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Wolf of Wall Street?
faq.answer: The Wolf of Wall Street (2013) is best described as Crime, Drama, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Wolf of Wall Street worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.0/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
